an5.51:1.2: On one occasion the Blessed One was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
an5.51:1.5: “Yes, lord,” the monks responded to him.
an5.51:2.1: “These five are obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment.
an5.51:2.3: “Sensual desire is an obstacle, a hindrance that overwhelms awareness and weakens discernment.
an5.51:2.7: Uncertainty is an obstacle, a hindrance that overwhelms awareness and weakens discernment.
an5.51:2.8: These are the five obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment.
an5.51:3.1: And when a monk has not abandoned these five obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment, when he is without strength and weak in discernment: For him to understand what is for his own benefit, to understand what is for the benefit of others, to understand what is for the benefit of both, to realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction in knowledge & vision—that is impossible.
an5.51:3.2: “Suppose there were a river, flowing down from the mountains—going far, its current swift, carrying everything with it—
an5.51:3.3: and a man would open channels leading away from it on both sides,
an5.51:3.4: so that the current in the middle of the river would be dispersed, diffused, & dissipated; it wouldn’t go far, its current wouldn’t be swift, and it wouldn’t carry everything with it.
an5.51:3.5: In the same way, when a monk has not abandoned these five obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment, when he is without strength and weak in discernment: For him to understand what is for his own benefit, to understand what is for the benefit of others, to understand what is for the benefit of both, to realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction in knowledge & vision—that is impossible.
an5.51:4.1: “Now, when a monk has abandoned these five obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment, when he is strong in discernment: For him to understand what is for his own benefit, to understand what is for the benefit of others, to understand what is for the benefit of both, to realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction in knowledge & vision—that is possible.
an5.51:4.2: “Suppose there were a river, flowing down from the mountains—going far, its current swift, carrying everything with it—
an5.51:4.3: and a man would close the channels leading away from it on both sides,
an5.51:4.4: so that the current in the middle of the river would be undispersed, undiffused, & undissipated; it would go far, its current swift, carrying everything with it.
an5.51:4.5: In the same way, when a monk has abandoned these five obstacles, hindrances that overwhelm awareness and weaken discernment, when he is strong in discernment: For him to understand what is for his own benefit, to understand what is for the benefit of others, to understand what is for the benefit of both, to realize a superior human state, a truly noble distinction in knowledge & vision—that is possible.”
